article.title: Elon Musk: Government Checks Are 'Best Way' to Deal With AI Job Losses
article.summary:
Elon Musk thinks he has a solution to AI-driven job losses: the government pays for everyone to live well.
Loading audio narration..."Universal HIGH INCOME via checks issued by the Federal government is the best way to deal with unemployment caused by AI," the Tesla and SpaceX CEO said in a late Thursday post on X.
Universal HIGH INCOME via checks issued by the Federal government is the best way to deal with unemployment caused by AI.
"For those who do lose their jobs, retraining done well preserves agency and self-worth in ways a basic income cannot," he added.
Experts in personal finance and AI told Business Insider that Musk's message was "dangerous and misleading," and emphasized that even if AI delivers abundance, governments will have to effectively distribute the gains.

article.title: Faisal Islam: What people in power think the impact of the Iran war will be
article.summary:
While there is still much uncertainty from the Gulf, the clouds are less dark now, allowing some to focus on other concerns.
Reeves could even be hopeful after growth figures showed the UK on course for 0.5% to 0.6% growth in the first quarter.
As news emerged yesterday of the reopening of the Strait, energy prices tumbled.
So too did borrowing costs, petrol prices, and mortgage rates.

article.title: The Trump team’s puzzling ‘all is well’ message on the economy
article.summary:
It was a puzzling and dicey political message before the war; it’s even more puzzling and dicey now.
Today, the message is more: Despite what voters are hearing (or seeing at any local gas station), things are actually quite good.
“We had the best economy in the history of our country my first term,” Trump added.
And perhaps more to the point, Americans said 65%-25% that Trump had worsened economic conditions rather than made them better.
The war has clearly made the economy more of a liability for Trump.

article.title: How Will Markets React to Strait of Hormuz Being Closed Again?
article.summary:
After the announcement of the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz on Friday, the Dow went up nearly 900 points, and the S&P 500 and Nasdaq also rose sharply.
It comes as oil prices also plunged which might mean gas prices will start coming down as well.
NBC’s Allie Canal joins Saturday TODAY to break down what Iran’s new closure of the Strait of Hormuz means for the economy, gas prices and more.

article.title: 6,000 Meters Under the Pacific, Japan Seeks Independence From China on Rare Earths
article.summary:
The feat is set to strengthen Japan's role in the increasingly crucial rare earths sector, a central element in the trade war between China and the United States.
Rare earths are a group of 17 metals critical to advanced technologies.
The embargo caused panic across industries, particularly in the automotive sector, and global prices of rare earths increased tenfold within a year.
The Japanese government has created strategic reserves of rare earths to mitigate any temporary supply disruptions.
Japan is not only an importer of rare earths, but an advanced player in their transformation into high-value-added components.

The war with Iran has yet to produce any discernible increase in food prices.
So, it takes time for an energy shock to work its way through the supply chain.
So the impact of rising diesel costs may not work its way into that part of the supply chain for weeks.
So, when they pass through the supply chain, consumers can substitute: If beef gets more expensive, they can eat more chicken.

article.title: Gas prices are sky-high. Here are 8 ways to save at the pump.

Changing gas prices are reshaping American drivers' everyday lives, whether through cutting back on trips or shaking up their budgets.

Check on your vehicleDrivers saving money on gas "really comes down to keeping their vehicle fuel efficient and running properly," Bennett said.

article.title: OnePlus' Europe exit isn't official yet, but the signs aren't great

Reports indicate OnePlus is reviewing its global strategy, possibly exiting key markets to focus on China.
It seems not all is going well with OnePlus, as a new LinkedIn post from a OnePlus employee in Europe suggests that the company may be scaling down its operations in the region.
A Reddit post highlighted a now-deleted LinkedIn update from a senior community manager at OnePlus Europe, suggesting that the company may be exiting the market.
The company has launched new devices, but multiple reports indicate it may exit certain global markets to focus more on its China business.

article.title: How New Grads Can Stand Out in a Slower Job Market
article.summary:
At the same time, Chan said, there are more "hidden job market opportunities," where grads land roles through networking rather than by solely applying through job boards.
Show your workChan said entry-level jobs aren't going away, though the baseline has shifted.
Now that they've been exposed to it for years, it's important for grads to show they know how to use AI, Chan said.
Grads also need to show they could use AI in entry-level work "so that they can actually add value more quickly," he said.
Many entry-level roles, rather than going away, are instead "infused with this need for AI and discussion of AI," he said.

article.title: How 2 couples cut housing costs to speed up their path to early retirement
article.summary:
Paying off the loan also significantly reduced their fixed costs, helping accelerate their path to financial independence.
Early retirees Kristy Shen and Bryce Leung are the founders of The Millennial Revolution.
"We didn't upgrade our housing," Shen said.
Though their strategies differed, both couples focused on housing to reduce one of the highest costs in any budget.
Cutting those major recurring costs can free up far more money than skipping lattes or canceling a few subscriptions.

article.title: Iran says it has closed the Strait of Hormuz again, as ceasefire nears its end

Despite hinting at the possibility of resuming attacks on Iran, Trump also told reporters "I think it's going to happen," referring to a deal.
Hezbollah, meanwhile, urged people displaced by the fighting not to rush back to southern Lebanon, the Bekaa Valley and Beirut's southern suburbs until the situation becomes clearer.
The latest round of fighting began after Hezbollah fired rockets into northern Israel following the U.S. and Israeli attacks on Iran on Feb. 28.
Israeli forces responded with airstrikes and a ground invasion of southern Lebanon.

article.title: Anthropic’s new cybersecurity model could get it back in the government’s good graces
article.summary:
The Trump administration has spent nearly two months fighting with AI company Anthropic.
But some of the ice may reportedly be melting between the two, thanks to Anthropic’s buzzy new cybersecurity-focused model: Claude Mythos Preview.
Anthropic has recently attempted to get back in the US government’s good graces, at least in some capacity, with Mythos Preview.
The release of Mythos Preview has already reportedly sparked emergency meetings between US bank leaders and Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell.
If Amodei’s meeting opens up conversations about further integrating Anthropic’s Claude into government usage across agencies, it’s possible that the DoD could shift its views on Claude accordingly as well.

article.title: AI Startup Are Moving Into Smaller Neighborhoods to Suit Their Culture
article.summary:
Despite the pandemic, an exodus of tech companies, and a feared "doom loop," a one-two punch of return-to-office mandates and the explosion of AI startups has shifted San Francisco's tech center into smaller neighborhoods.
Companies focused on AI, such as Virtual AI, Applied Compute, Rox AI, and Curai Health, are all located in the same building as Together AI.
Other AI companies with a three-block radius include Scale AI, Resolve AI, and Gamma.
"An office district isn't an office district anymore — it's a mixed-use area," said Florida.
Between the Mission District and the Castro, several up-and-coming AI companies have also quietly set up offices along the main street, including Comp AI and Ace AI, just a few blocks away from the AI Start Academy.

article.title: I've lived in China for over 10 years and saw the OpenClaw craze unfold. The West needs to keep up.
article.summary:
This as-told-to essay is based on a conversation with Thomas Derksen, a German entrepreneur who has lived in China for over a decade.
There's so much happening in China, and I'm always interested in how things are different in the West and in Asia.
People in China are more curious, and they have a strong business mindset.
Some people don't think normal jobs will give them a good future, so they try OpenClaw to build something.
In China, a crayfish shop owner sees a tool and thinks: Maybe I can use this to grow my business.

article.title: This charming gadget writes bad AI poetry
article.summary:
I’ve never been as charmed and frustrated by one gadget as I have with the Poetry Camera.
I tried to set this aside and accept the Poetry Camera free of bias, but maybe I was just never going to have a good time with it.
The Poetry Camera puts together words that kind of sound deep and meaningful on the surface, but also feel soulless and read like empty calories.
AI can be a powerful tool in making software, but writing meaningful poetry requires, at a bare minimum, a soul.
I’m still not sure what Poetry Camera is, but I do know one thing: it’s not for me.

article.title: How Big Wall Street Banks, From JPMorgan to Citi, Are Using AI
article.summary:
Wall Street is betting big on, and pouring billions into, AI — and leaders have had to answer for their spending.
During banks' first-quarter earnings last week, analysts pressed some executives on how those giant investments are paying off, whether in productivity gains or head count changes.
April's earnings calls came just one week after many Wall Street CEOs attended an impromptu meeting at the Treasury to discuss the powerful new AI model.
As banks rapidly weave generative AI into everything from trading floors to back offices, the technology is reshaping not just workflows, but workplace culture.
Here's what we know about how Wall Street banks are embracing AI.

article.title: Baumgartner Introduces Bipartisan Bill to Tighten Controls on Sensitive Chipmaking Equipment
article.summary:
Baumgartner Introduces Bipartisan Bill to Tighten Controls on Sensitive Chipmaking Equipment | Press ReleasesWASHINGTON – Congressman Michael Baumgartner (WA‑05) introduced the Multilateral Alignment of Technology Controls on Hardware (MATCH) Act, a bipartisan bill designed to strengthen U.S. national security by closing critical gaps in export controls on semiconductor manufacturing equipment (SME).
For too long, our export controls have been a patchwork of entity-based restrictions that Beijing easily bypasses using front companies.
When foreign suppliers of advanced chipmaking tools are not subject to the same rules as U.S. firms, it undermines the objectives of U.S. export controls.
Allied export controls on semiconductor manufacturing equipment suffer from meaningful gaps that China continues to exploit.
We must ensure our allies align their export controls with American standards — through diplomacy, if possible, but unilaterally if necessary.

article.title: What is Mythos and why are experts worried about Anthropic’s AI model
article.summary:
Instead of a public rollout, Anthropic is using its Project Glasswing initiative to offer a small group of organizations access to its Mythos AI model for cybersecurity testing.
Not since OpenAI temporarily withheld its GPT-2 model in 2019 has a major developer deemed a system too dangerous for the public.
It also scored 31 percentage points higher than Anthropic’s previous cutting-edge model, Opus 4.6, on the USAMO 2026 Mathematical Olympiad, a grueling, two-day proof-based competition.
Prior to April 2025, no AI model could complete those tasks at all.
Neither expert denies that Mythos is a significant advance, but suggest the decisive regulatory action is partly driven by institutional self-preservation.

article.title: Dairy Queen is putting an AI chatbot in its drive-thrus
article.summary:
Dairy Queen is becoming the latest fast food chain to get in on AI, as it’s bringing a chatbot to dozens of its drive-thrus across the US and Canada.
It aims to help speed up drive-thru service and “encourage customers to add more food to their orders,” according to The Wall Street Journal.
Following a test last year, Dairy Queen is widely launching the tech built by Presto, an AI company that already works with chains like Carl’s Jr., Hardee’s, Taco John’s, and Fazoli’s.
In 2023, a report from Bloomberg revealed that Presto’s AI drive-thrus may actually be assisted by human workers based in locations like the Philippines.
Presto’s chatbot takes orders correctly about 90 percent of the time, The Wall Street Journal reports.

article.summary:

Meanwhile, TikTok (77) and YouTube (78) both scored better than the AI platforms.
Gemini is one of the most prolific AI services, with access via smart speakers, TVs, phones and computers, while most ChatGPT users access the AI tool via the ChatGPT website or mobile app, and Grok via social media platform X.
21% reported an "extremely favorable" outlook toward AI, while an equal 21% said they are "very concerned about the consequences."

Sora was a project that could not have happened anywhere but OpenAI, and I will always deeply love this place for that.

article.title: 3 top executives leave OpenAI in a single day
article.summary:
OpenAI lost three top executives on Friday.
Bill Peebles, who headed OpenAI's AI video app Sora, also announced his departure.
Although Peebles didn't explain why in his post, OpenAI shut down Sora last month due to cost and compute constraints.
Prism, an AI workplace for scientists that Weil oversaw, is moving to Codex, OpenAI's AI developer assistant, which is expanding beyond coding.
The shakeup comes as OpenAI narrows its focus by cutting back on "side quests" and doubling down on selling to businesses.

article.title: Apollo's president warns the AI spending boom may not pay off for investors
article.summary:
Tech companies are pouring trillions into artificial intelligence, but investors shouldn't assume the bets will pay off, Jim Zelter, president of Apollo Global Management, said.
There's no doubt they're going to have a massive utility," Zelter said on Goldman Sachs' "Exchanges" podcast published on Thursday.
Zelter said investors shouldn't treat equity-like risk as if it were safe, fixed-income exposure.
Howard Marks, the cofounder of Oaktree Capital Management, said in December that too many investors have a "lottery-ticket mentality" when it comes to AI.
But they're still spending: Nearly 80% of the CEOs told KPMG they plan to allocate at least 5% of capital to AI this year.

article.title: Unmanned submarine detects eerie structure under Antarctica, then loses contact
article.summary:
A University of Gothenburg unmanned submarine, named Ran, sent back peculiar images of step-like and teardrop-shaped structures before disappearing under a West Antarctic ice shelf in 2024.
The features that were mapped — resembling shapes humans would make — aren't evidence of Atlantis, but rather of significant ice melt that continues to draw expert focus to the area.
The eerie structures imaged by Ran are the result of melting that's undercutting the Dotson shelf, creating an unstable overbite.
When shelves break off, they no longer brace the land-based glaciers behind them, accelerating ice movement and sea level rise, according to Earth.com.
Antarctic ice melt has contributed just over half an inch to sea levels since 1979, according to Earth.com, referencing the satellite and climate information.

article.title: Now Brief for your Samsung TV is a reality with this SmartThings update
article.summary:
Soon, users with a Samsung TV from 2024 or later will find Now Brief on their displays with a recap of the night.
Samsung is rolling out its newest SmartThings update, and the company is highlighting a feature that's found a new home: Now Brief.
The Galaxy S26 series is seemingly spearheading this feature's debut, as Samsung says Now Brief will support "Home Security, Family Care, and Pet Care."
Android Central's Take This Family Care update was pretty interesting.
In particular, it says Care Insights delivers more actionable and long-term data within the Family Care environment.

article.title: Common cleaning sponge found to release trillions of microplastic fibers
article.summary:
As these sponges wear down, they can release tiny plastic particles into the environment.
Researchers estimate that more than a trillion microplastic fibers may be released worldwide every month as people use and discard these everyday cleaning tools.
These fragments can break down further into microplastic fibers, which are tiny strands of plastic often too small to see.
Denser sponges held up better over time and released fewer microplastic fibers, while less dense versions broke down more quickly.
Using Amazon sales from August 2023 as a reference point, they calculated that approximately 1.55 trillion microplastic fibers could be released each month.

article.title: Sharks and tuna are overheating and running out of options
article.summary:
As ocean temperatures climb, these species are more likely to overheat, which could shrink their habitable range and push them toward cooler regions closer to the poles.
What Makes "Mesothermic" Fish UniqueThe study, published in the journal Science, focuses on "mesothermic" fishes.
These are a rare group, making up less than 0.1% of all fish species, that can retain body heat and keep parts of themselves warmer than the surrounding water.
These thresholds represent the water temperatures at which large fish can no longer release heat quickly enough to maintain stable body temperatures.
Looking ahead, the researchers expect that suitable habitats for large mesothermic fish will shrink as global temperatures continue to rise, especially during warmer months.

article.title: Apple Says CarPlay Ultra is Coming to These Vehicle Brands

In May 2025, Apple said many other vehicle brands planned to offer CarPlay Ultra, including Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis.

article.title: Greenland ice completely melted 7,000 years ago and could happen again
article.summary:
A new study from GreenDrill -- a project co-led by the University at Buffalo to recover rock and sediment buried beneath the Greenland Ice Sheet -- has revealed that the Prudhoe Dome ice cap completely vanished about 7,000 years ago.
The findings, published in Nature Geoscience, show that this elevated region in the northwest part of the ice sheet is highly sensitive to even modest warming.
Their chemical signatures reveal when the surface was last exposed to sunlight, helping pinpoint past periods when the ice sheet disappeared.
By identifying weak points along the edges of the Greenland Ice Sheet, scientists can better predict where melting may begin and which coastal regions could face the greatest risk.
Instead, they recovered sediment that later helped scientists discover the ice sheet had been much smaller around 400,000 years ago.

article.title: Comparing 12 VDC Air Fryers With Regular 240 VAC Ones
article.summary:
That boiling water is a contentious topic of discussion is clear, but what about hot air?
As scientific test samples chicken nuggets were used with the test, following a preheating period for the 12 VDC unit.
Meanwhile the 240 VAC unit burned through 250 Wh in 20 minutes, with no pre-heating, though only 230 Wh with no inverter losses included.
As a final test, the 12 VDC unit was run at 400 Watt using 14.6 VDC input, which did indeed get it up to temperature much faster.
This mirrors the results with boiling water, where most options mostly vary in how much time they require to get water up to a boiling temperature.

article.title: Indie App Spotlight: ‘QuakeInfo’ is a fast and easy way to monitor ongoing earthquakes
article.summary:
Welcome to Indie App Spotlight.
This is a weekly 9to5Mac series where we showcase the latest apps in the indie app world.
Top featuresUsing data from both US and European databases, QuakeInfo can show you seismic activity from around the world.
It also has a comprehensive list view, giving you info on each individual earthquake, down to the timing, magnitude, location, and depth.
QuakeInfo provides location-based safety warnings, spatial context, and detailed metadata on each individual earthquake – so you know what’s coming before it potentially hits you.

article.title: Acting ICE director says he's resigning amid record-breaking shutdown
article.summary:
Acting ICE director says he's resigning amid record-breaking shutdowntoggle caption Tom Brenner/APPresident Trump's top Immigration and Customs Enforcement official, Todd Lyons, said he would resign on Thursday as acting director, adding to the list of leadership shakeups at the Department of Homeland Security.
Under his tenure, the agency took the lead in Trump's mass deportation agenda, rapidly scaling up sweeping arrests across the country.
Madison Sheahan, former deputy director at Immigration and Customs Enforcement, left her post at the start of the year to run for Congress.
The ICE director must be confirmed by the Senate; Lyons had only been serving in an acting capacity.
There has not been a Senate-confirmed ICE director since the Obama administration.

article.title: How Americans really feel about immigration
article.summary:
Immigration enforcement was once one of President Donald Trump’s strongest issues, driving his victories in the 2016 and 2024 presidential contests.
But these days, most Americans seem to hate just about everything Trump’s administration has done to actually address the issue.
Polls show Americans have shifted dramatically on immigration since Trump returned to office — and about half of all Americans now want to abolish ICE, the deportation force Trump has empowered since returning to office.
But what do Americans really think about immigration, beyond their feelings about the current administration?
Not only can this constituency not vote, but Americans generally tend to really underestimate, I think, how interconnected we all are with the immigration system.

The student workers cover a wide range of hospitality and tourism roles, including lifeguards, bartenders, theme park attendants, retail staffers, and restaurant servers.

As he drove in an unmarked SUV, Morgan encountered rush-hour traffic, which he decided to bypass by unlawfully driving on the highway’s shoulder.

article.title: Record number of Rohingya refugees died at sea last year, UNHCR says
article.summary:
The UN refugee agency has warned of an ‘unmarked graveyard for thousands of desperate Rohingya refugees’ in South AsiaThe United Nations refugee agency has revealed that nearly 900 Rohingya refugees were reported dead or missing in the Bay of Bengal and the Andaman Sea in 2025.
Speaking to reporters in Geneva, the UNHCR’s spokesperson, Babar Baloch, described the area as an “unmarked graveyard for thousands of desperate Rohingya refugees”, noting that some 5,000 are thought to have drowned at sea over the last decade.
Hundreds of thousands of Rohingya refugees began fleeing Myanmar in 2017 amid an ethnic cleansing campaign.
They largely settled in refugee camps in Bangladesh, which continues to give refuge to those fleeing today.
AdvertisementDeadly journeysEarlier this month, an overcrowded trawler carrying about 250 Rohingya refugees and Bangladeshi nationals sank in the Andaman Sea.

But this is not how they expected to grow old: Squatting in a vacant building in central Beirut, displaced many times.

article.title: FAA Lifts Blanket Aerial Ban It Placed to Protect ICE Activity From Aerial Scrutiny
article.summary:
In January, the National Press Photographers’ Association (NPPA) urged the FAA to withdraw sweeping drone restrictions that were meant to make it more difficult for journalists to cover ICE activity.
This “moving ban” as it was called came into effect in January and was scheduled to stay in place through October 2027.
“Our members are finding it impossible to comply with the order—even when photographing stories with drones that are unrelated to these agencies,” NPPA President Alex Garcia said in January.
“The rescission of the broad and unconstitutional flight restriction means that drone journalists will no longer have to risk their drone license and criminal charges because of an overbroad, invisible, moving flight restriction.
NPPA is grateful for all of those who advocated against the ill-conceived TFR.”Image credits: Header photo licensed via Depositphotos.com.

article.title: Palestinian football officials worry Canada won't approve visas in time for FIFA meeting
article.summary:
Media reports published this week suggested that Palestinian soccer officials were barred from entering Canada ahead of the FIFA Congress slated to take place in Vancouver later this month.
The Guardian reported Thursday that three unnamed officials from the Palestinian Football Association (PFA) "have had applications for visas to enter Canada rejected" ahead of the 76th FIFA Congress in Vancouver on April 30.
Jibril Rajoub, head of the Palestinian Football Association, speaks during a media conference in the Israeli-occupied West Bank city of Ramallah in September 2018.
"Hundreds of Palestinian players, officials and children of Palestinian football academies — who were part of this football community — have been brutally killed."
(Fernando Calistro/The Associated Press)Kat Vilarev, a sports law expert familiar with the situation, told CBC that the PFA's attendance at the FIFA Congress is key.

article.title: Pakistan PM, army chief wrap up key trips in push for more US-Iran talks
article.summary:
Field Marshal Asim Munir leaves Tehran while premier Shehbaz Sharif heads home from Turkiye amid hopes of another round of US-Iran talks.
Munir held talks with the country’s president, foreign minister, parliament speaker and head of Iran’s military central command centre.
But diplomacy has continued, with Pakistan’s Prime Minister, Shehbaz Sharif, visiting Saudi Arabia, Qatar and Turkiye to push the peace process.
Donald Trump has said a second round of talks with Iran could be held in Pakistan in the coming days.
Reporting from Islamabad, Al Jazeera’s Kamal Hyder said Munir landed back home on Saturday as Pakistan prepared for another round of US-Iran talks expected “within the next few days”.

article.title: Iran war: What is happening on day 50 of the US-Iran conflict?
article.summary:
The Strait was reopened earlier on Saturday subject to conditions, in line with the Lebanon ceasefire and previously coordinated maritime routes.
In the GulfAdvertisementFragile recovery: Saudi Finance Minister Mohammed Al-Jadaan welcomes Iran reopening the Strait of Hormuz, but says the situation remains fragile.
In IsraelIsrael ‘prohibited’ from striking Lebanon: Trump says Israel is barred by the US from continuing attacks on Lebanon, declaring “enough is enough”.
Lebanon war ‘last anti-colonial battle’: Analyst Rami Khouri says the conflict reflects resistance by Iran-aligned groups against decades of Western influence, with southern Lebanon at its core.
Boeing’s war boost: The war has so far boosted Boeing’s defence business and hasn’t affected deliveries to airline customers facing high jet fuel prices, the company’s CEO said.

article.title: Some ships are sailing through the Strait of Hormuz. Others are turning around. Here's what 'completely open' looks like for now.
article.summary:
Iran announced Friday morning that the Strait of Hormuz is open for maritime traffic, and mid-afternoon data showed that commercial vessels were passing through the strategic waterway, though only a small number.
MarineTraffic data from Friday afternoon showed that just over half a dozen commercial vessels had transited the strait since the announcement.
Data from KPler, a real-time trade intelligence platform, showed that eight commercial vessels had crossed as of 2 pm EST Friday compared to five on Thursday.
But by the evening, new data showed ships were turning back.
Earlier in the week, days before the announcement on the strait, US Navy warships set up a blockade of Iranian ships and ports.

article.title: World reacts to Strait of Hormuz reopening amid US-Iran conflict
article.summary:
Iran’s Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi and United States President Donald Trump have said that the Strait of Hormuz is open to commercial vessels.
Trump affirmed on social media that the strait was open, later claiming that Iran had agreed to “never close the Strait of Hormuz again”.
He said he wanted US involvement in the international mission to secure shipping through the Strait of Hormuz.
FinlandFinnish President Alexander Stubb, who participated in the Paris summit, said on X, “We welcome Iran’s announcement on opening the Strait.
Since the outbreak of the conflict, we have followed the guidance of our security partners in the region, and the recommendation so far has been to avoid transiting the Strait of Hormuz.

article.title: This Ukrainian counter-drone company is worried about missing its moment in the Iran war
article.summary:
A Ukrainian interceptor drone taking flight.
Much of Ukraine's defense industry says exports would actually boost domestic production.
One Ukrainian defense industry leader told Business Insider that interceptor exports could ultimately mean more interceptors for Ukraine's military.
He added that joint production with allied countries — already growing — could benefit both partners and Ukraine's war effort.
A Ukrainian interceptor drone, which typically costs $6,000 or less, is a more appealing and effective option.

article.title: Iran foreign minister says Strait of Hormuz ‘completely open’
article.summary:
Iran says ‘passage for all commercial vessels through Strait of Hormuz’ open during the Israel-Lebanon ceasefire.
Iran has said the Strait of Hormuz is “completely open” for all commercial vessels and will remain so during the Israel-Lebanon ceasefire, as United States President Donald Trump said an agreement to end the US-Israeli war on Iran was “very close”.
“In line with the ceasefire in Lebanon, the passage for all commercial vessels through Strait of Hormuz is declared completely open for the remaining period of ceasefire,” Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said in a post on X on Friday.
He said in a separate post that Iran had agreed to “never close the Strait of Hormuz again”.
The conflict saw Iran block traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, through which a fifth of the world’s oil and liquefied natural gas transits.

article.title: Iran war: What is happening on day 49 of the US-Iran conflict?
article.summary:
Celebrations in Lebanon as a 10-day ceasefire begins, opening the door to talks on a longer-term Israel-Lebanon deal.
Trump said an agreement to end the war on Iran is near, with the next round of talks possibly this weekend in Islamabad.
‘No sticking points’ in Iran talks: The US president said a deal is “very close” and could be “very good for everybody”.
Trump hopes Hezbollah ‘acts nicely’: Trump said on Thursday he hopes Hezbollah “acts nicely and well” during the 10-day truce.
Hezbollah in ceasefire: Trump said the truce between Israel and Lebanon would include Iran-backed Hezbollah.

article.title: Turkiye woos investors amid Iran war fallout in Gulf economies
article.summary:
Turkish officials are promoting Istanbul as a regional financial hub amid the war’s fallout on the Gulf economies.
As the fallout of the war has reverberated across the Middle East, Ankara has jumped at the chance to promote Turkiye as a model of security and stability for businesses and investors.
“A liberal investment environment, ease of entry and new comprehensive incentive packages should help boost its position,” Bagis told Al Jazeera.
“There is a particularly strong strategic focus from Far Eastern institutions,” the spokesperson told Al Jazeera.
“In emerging financial systems, investor confidence is primarily driven by predictability, transparency,” Dincer told Al Jazeera.
